name: OpenADE / ADE App

runner.notes: Install from upstream docs, prepare a run worktree, provide prompt.md exactly, then run harness verification.

inclusion: Direct open-source ADE competitor focused on local agentic planning and execution.
pricing_model: Free/open-source app; bring-your-own Claude Code or Codex/API access.
public_stack_signals: README states free/open-source, local/offline, powered by Claude Code and Codex.
Repository metadata identifies TypeScript as primary language.
README describes projects/web and projects/electron development flow, TypeScript throughout, and MobX state management.
setup_notes: Retain ade-app.dev as a legacy roster URL, but use openade.ai and GitHub OpenADE as current official sources.

sources.notes: Original roster URL; retained as legacy/alternate ADE App landing source.
README, license badge, feature list, platform downloads, and implementation signals.

research.positioning: Open-source local ADE that makes agents plan, revise, and then execute, with comments and snapshots around the workflow.

backend: local agent harnesses for Claude Code and Codex
packaging: Windows installer marked experimental in README
unknowns: Internal persistence format and extension/plugin boundary need source inspection beyond README.
Windows maturity is experimental according to README.

infrastructure: 100% local/offline claim: no cloud and no sync for code according to README.
Uses user-provided Claude Code or Codex access as execution engine.
Every execution creates a git patch snapshot according to README.

review_shipping: comment on files, diffs, changes, and agent messages
remote_mobile: no mobile or remote fleet feature mapped in official sources reviewed

parity_risks: Plan-first workflow may need prompts that ask for plan, revision, and execution without adding extra hints.
No-direct-editing anti-feature changes how manual intervention should be counted.
Windows is experimental in upstream README.

hypotheses: Plan-first flow may improve correctness on tasks where prompt interpretation is the main risk.
Local/offline and free/open-source distribution lower adoption friction.
Automated git snapshots and rollback are valuable for repeatable experiments.
evidence: README states Plan -> Revise -> Do, HyperPlan, comments on files/diffs/messages, local/offline, git snapshots, worktrees, and built-in diff/file/terminal/process manager.
README states OpenADE is free/open-source and powered by Claude Code and Codex.
unknowns: How well plan reconciliation translates to final benchmark correctness remains untested.
